resource: Right to feast in the Prytaneum
verbatim: ['But the author, whoever he was, of the play called The Beggars, which is ascribed to Chionides, says that the Athenians, when they place a banquet for Castor and Pollux in their Prytaneum, serve up on the tables cheese and barley-cakes, and olives which have fallen, and leeks, for the sake of reminding people of the ancient manner of living. And Solon enjoins them to serve up barley-cakes to those who eat in the prytaneum: and besides that, to place bread on the table at festivals, in imitation of Homer . . . And Ctesicles, in the third book of his Chronicles, says that in the hundred and fifteenth Olympiad, there was an investigation at Athens conducted by Demetrius Phalereus into the number of the inhabitants of Attica, and the Athenians were found to amount to twenty-one thousand, and the Metics to ten thousand, and the slaves to four hundred thousand.']
reasoning: Solon's laws restricted the right to feast at the public expense (in the Prytaneum) to specific citizens and officials, excluding the resident alien (metic) population despite their significant number in the census mentioned.
materiality_reasoning: Food distributions provide tangible means of subsistence.
generality_reasoning: Narrowly scoped to a specific institution (Prytaneum) and food types.
persistence_reasoning: The right to public maintenance is often a lifelong/recurring privilege.
immutability_reasoning: Metic status is a durable social identity of foreign residency.
contemporarity_reasoning: Cites Solon and archaic/classical Athenian history.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Attests to statutes (Solon) and census figures as facts.
tautology_reasoning: Exclusion from public dining is not the definition of a metic.

verbatim: ['And on all the other days of the year it is lawful for any one who pleases of those who are fed at the prytaneum to go into the prytaneum to sup, having prepared at his own home some vegetable, or some pulse, or some salt meat, or some fish, or a very little bit of pork; and when he eats this, he may also have a cotyla of wine. But no woman is allowed to go into the prytaneum excepting the woman alone who plays the flute. And no spoon may be brought into the prytaneum.']
reasoning: The text describes a contemporary rule in Naucratis where women are strictly barred from entering the communal dining hall (prytaneum), with a single narrow exception for a musician, who is likely not considered a guest.
materiality_reasoning: Access to a public dining hall involves tangible food and wine.
generality_reasoning: Specific but recurring access to a central civic institution.
persistence_reasoning: Exclusion was a lifelong structural restriction based on gender.
immutability_reasoning: Sex/gender is biologically fixed.
contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the laws and customs currently in force in Naucratis.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Stated as a direct rule: 'no woman is allowed to go'.
tautology_reasoning: Female gender does not inherently mean 'excluded from the prytaneum'.

verbatim: ['And I have always, O my friends, marvelled at the race of slaves, considering how abstemious they are, though placed in the middle of such numbers of dainties; for they pass them by, not only out of fear, but also because they are taught to do so . . . as in the island of Cos, when the citizens sacrifice to Juno. For Macareus says, in his third book of his treatise on Coan Affairs, that, when the Coans sacrifice to Juno, no slave is allowed to enter the temple, nor does any slave taste any one of the things which are prepared for the sacrifice.']
reasoning: In Coan society, religious law specifically prohibits slaves from entering temples during certain sacrifices or consuming the sacred food, a resource reserved for citizens.
materiality_reasoning: Food and temple entry have tangible material and social dimensions.
generality_reasoning: Recurring religious exclusion from specific civic sacrifices.
persistence_reasoning: Slavery is a lifelong status with lifelong religious exclusions.
immutability_reasoning: Slave status is a structural legal birth/status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Cites Macareus' historical/antiquarian treatise on Coan history.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Direct report of a prohibition: 'no slave is allowed'.
tautology_reasoning: Religious exclusion is a specific legal disability, not the definition of a slave.

verbatim: ['And Phorbas was so delighted with this hospitality, that when he was dying himself he charged his friends to take care that his funeral rites should be performed by free men. And so this custom continued to prevail in the sacrifice of Phorbas, for none but free men minister at this sacrifice. And it is accounted profanation for any slave to approach it.']
reasoning: The text identifies a specific cult rule in Rhodes where slaves are deemed a source of profanation and are legally barred from participating in or even approaching the sacrifice honoring the hero Phorbas.
materiality_reasoning: Ministering at a rite is a social role with symbolic payoff.
generality_reasoning: Tied to a specific local cult of a named hero (Phorbas).
persistence_reasoning: The exclusion holds for the entirety of the individual's life as a slave.
immutability_reasoning: Slave status is a structural legal status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to a cultic legend and local custom in Rhodes.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports it as a prevailing religious custom/law.
tautology_reasoning: Ritual profanation is not the definition of a slave.

verbatim: ['And among the Romans no slave ever drank wine, nor any free woman, nor any youth born of free parents till he was thirty years of age.']
reasoning: The text describes an ancient Roman sumptuary and social law that restricted access to wine based on both freedom and age criteria.
materiality_reasoning: Wine is a tangible physical good.
generality_reasoning: Sumptuary law regarding a specific broad category of consumption.
persistence_reasoning: For slaves, this was a lifelong prohibition.
immutability_reasoning: Slave status is a structural legal status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to ancient Roman social history/customs.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reported as a general historical fact of the Roman state.
tautology_reasoning: Abstinence from wine is not the definition of a slave.

verbatim: ['But among the Romans, as Polybius says, in his sixth book, it was forbidden to women to drink wine at all. However, they drink what is called Passum; and that is made of raisins . . . And it is impossible for a woman to drink wine without being detected: for, first of all, she has not the key of the cellar; and, in the next place, she is bound to kiss her relations, and those of her husband, down to cousins, and do this every day when she first sees them . . . for if she has merely tasted wine, it needs no informer, but is sure to betray itself.']
reasoning: The text details a Roman law (and the biological/social checks used to enforce it) that prohibited women from consuming wine, though they were allowed a raisin-based alternative.
materiality_reasoning: Wine is a tangible physical good.
generality_reasoning: Sumptuary law regarding a specific broad category of consumption.
persistence_reasoning: Lifelong prohibition for the gender group.
immutability_reasoning: Sex/gender is biologically fixed.
contemporarity_reasoning: Cites Polybius regarding archaic/historical Roman law.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Details specific legal enforcement mechanisms as historical fact.
tautology_reasoning: Being a woman does not define one as a non-wine drinker.

verbatim: ['And among the Romans no slave ever drank wine, nor any free woman, nor any youth born of free parents till he was thirty years of age.']
reasoning: Historical Roman law established a specific age barrier (thirty years) before which freeborn males were prohibited from accessing wine.
materiality_reasoning: Wine is a tangible physical good.
generality_reasoning: Sumptuary law regarding a specific broad category of consumption.
persistence_reasoning: Bounded to the life stage of youth (until age thirty).
immutability_reasoning: Age is a biologically fixed status at a given time.
contemporarity_reasoning: Relates to historical Roman law.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reported as a categorical historical age-gate rule.
tautology_reasoning: One is not young because they don't drink wine.

verbatim: ['And every one contributes to the phiditium about three Attic semimedimni of meal, and about eleven or twelve choes of wine; and in addition to this they contributed a certain weight of cheese and figs; and moreover, for purchasing meat, they gave ten Aeginetan obols.']
reasoning: Spartan citizens were granted the right to participate in the 'phiditia' (communal meals) contingent upon a mandatory contribution of specific material resources (grain, wine, money).
materiality_reasoning: Meal, wine, and money for meat are tangible goods.
generality_reasoning: The Syssitia was a foundational axis of Spartan life.
persistence_reasoning: Long-term daily participation during the peak years of citizenship.
immutability_reasoning: Spartan citizenship (homoioi) was a structural birth-status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Narrates the classical Spartan institutional system.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Verbatim cites specific weights and measures for contributions.
tautology_reasoning: Contingent contribution is a requirement, not the definition of citizenship.

verbatim: ['And a certain man named Sophocles, passed a decree to banish all the philosophers from Attica. And Philo, the friend of Aristotle, wrote an oration against him; and Demochares, on the other hand, who was the cousin of Demosthenes, composed a defence for Sophocles.']
reasoning: The text records a specific Athenian legal decree that targeted individuals based on their occupation (philosophers) and revoked their right to inhabit the territory.
materiality_reasoning: Right of residence is a form of bodily entitlement/freedom of movement.
generality_reasoning: Expulsion from a territory is a broad/structural outcome.
persistence_reasoning: Banishment decrees are typically long-term or permanent till revoked.
immutability_reasoning: Occupation (philosopher) is acquired but is a durable social identity.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to a specific 4th-century BCE Athenian decree.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites a specific decree, the author, and the legal defense.
tautology_reasoning: Exile is not part of the definition of a philosopher.

verbatim: ['But when he had learned the power of his enemies and the misfortunes of his friends, although he was of opinion that he was being grossly wronged because they would not try him when he was in Athens but were for condemning him in his absence, not even in these circumstances did my father see fit to desert to the enemy;']
reasoning: The text describes the condemnation of Alcibiades in his absence as a 'gross wrong' and 'illegal,' implying a standard procedural rule in Athens that citizens have a right to be present at their own trial for such serious charges.
materiality_reasoning: Public trial is a concrete legal right with material bodily effects.
generality_reasoning: Specific but essential procedural legal protection.
persistence_reasoning: Holds for the duration of a single trial/case.
immutability_reasoning: Athenian citizenship is a structural birth-status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Describes events and laws in classical Athens (Alcibiades' time).
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Authorial framing of a legal standard to support a legal argument.
tautology_reasoning: Right to personal presence is a legal right, not a definition of a citizen.

verbatim: ['With regard to my father’s services here in Athens as choregus and gymnasiarch and trierarch—I am ashamed to speak; for so greatly did he excel in all the other public duties that, although those who have served the state in less splendid fashion sing their own praises therefor, if anyone should on my father’s behalf ask for a vote of thanks even in recognition of services as great as his, he would seem to be talking about petty things.']
reasoning: The mention of roles like choregus, gymnasiarch, and trierarch refers to the 'liturgies'—mandatory public services funded by the wealthiest citizens in Athens.
materiality_reasoning: Exemption from service costs involves significant private wealth.
generality_reasoning: Liturgies (trierarchy, etc.) were the main structural taxes for the elite.
persistence_reasoning: Obligation holds as long as one is in the 'wealthy' bracket.
immutability_reasoning: Wealth/property bracket is a durable but potentially changeable status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Recounting the classical liturgical system in Athens.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Refers to the roles (choregus, trierarch) as established institutions.
tautology_reasoning: Supporting a trireme is not what 'wealthy' means.

resource: Protection from prosecution for past political acts
verbatim: ['And then do you, Teisias, his brother-in-law and a member of the Council in the time of the Thirty Tyrants, have the hardihood to rake up old grudges against those of the other side, and are you not ashamed to be violating the terms of the amnesty which permits you to reside in the city']
reasoning: This refers to the historical Amnesty of 403 BCE following the civil war between the democrats and the Thirty Tyrants, which legally prevented citizens from being prosecuted for actions taken during the conflict to ensure social stability.
materiality_reasoning: Protection from past-act prosecution secures life/property outcomes.
generality_reasoning: Amnesty is a broad foundational legal protection for a whole class.
persistence_reasoning: Amnesty was intended as a permanent settlement of past guilt.
immutability_reasoning: Citizenship/factional residency is very hard to change legally.
contemporarity_reasoning: Discusses the Amnesty of 403 BCE shortly after it occurred.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites the 'terms of the amnesty' as a known established law.
tautology_reasoning: Amnesty for crimes is not part of the definition of citizenship.

verbatim: ['For although the same penalties are prescribed for all by our laws, yet the legal risk is not the same for all; on the contrary, the wealthy risk a fine, but those who are in straitened circumstances, as is the case with me, are in danger of disfranchisement, and this is a misfortune greater, in my opinion, than exile']
reasoning: In Athenian law, a citizen who could not pay a court-mandated fine (like the 5 talents mentioned) became a state debtor and suffered 'atimia' (loss of civic rights or disfranchisement).
materiality_reasoning: Citizenship (legal personhood) is a concrete legal status with material effects.
generality_reasoning: Legal personhood is a foundational societal axis.
persistence_reasoning: Disfranchisement (atimia) is typically permanent or long-term.
immutability_reasoning: The poor are a wealth bracket; durable but changeable.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to contemporary Athenian laws regarding unpaid fines and debts.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes the factual legal risk ('danger of disfranchisement') caused by specific laws.
tautology_reasoning: Being poor is not defined by being disfranchised.

verbatim: ['And when the men of the Piraeus were restored, and all the rest recovered their possessions, I alone by the influence of my personal enemies was deprived of the of the land which the people gave us as compensation for the confiscated property.']
reasoning: The speaker notes that after the restoration of the democracy, it was standard for citizens (except him, by exception) to recover possessions or receive land as compensation for property seized during the era of the Thirty Tyrants.
materiality_reasoning: Land and compensation for confiscated property are direct material wealth.
generality_reasoning: Property compensation is a broad legal right following civil unrest.
persistence_reasoning: Recovered land remains a permanent asset for the citizen.
immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a hard-to-change legal birth-status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Isocrates discusses laws active during his lifetime in post-restoration Athens.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Claims the law applied to 'all the rest' as a matter of fact.
tautology_reasoning: Being a citizen does not inherently define one as a victim of confiscation.

verbatim: ['Read to me also the law of Aegina; for it was necessary that the will be drawn in accordance with this law, since we were alien residents of this island.']
reasoning: The speaker explicitly states that as alien residents (metically-equivalent status) in Aegina, they were required and permitted to draw up their testaments according to the specific laws of that city-state to ensure validity.
materiality_reasoning: The right to execute a valid will controls the transmission of material wealth.
generality_reasoning: Testamentary rights are a major structural legal category.
persistence_reasoning: A valid will determines the lifelong afterlife of an estate.
immutability_reasoning: Resident alien (metic) status is durable but changeable by moving.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to contemporary Aeginetan law during a current trial.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim asks to read the actual statute as proof.
tautology_reasoning: Resident status does not define the specific local validity of a will.

verbatim: ['It was in accordance with this law, citizens of Aegina, that Thrasylochus adopted me as his son, for I was his fellow-citizen and friend... I therefore do not see how he could have acted more consistently with the law, since the law insists that persons of the same status may be adopted.']
reasoning: The text cites a specific legal requirement from the Aeginetan code (shared by Siphnos/Ceos) that for an adoption to be valid, the parties involved must share the same civic status (in this case, both being Siphnians).
materiality_reasoning: Adoption is a legal procedure with significant inheritance consequences.
generality_reasoning: Narrowly focused on the eligibility criteria for adoption.
persistence_reasoning: Adoption creates a permanent, lifelong familial and legal bond.
immutability_reasoning: Fellow-citizenship is a birth-fixed or hard-to-change legal status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Cites a law currently in force in Aegina/Siphnos.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Explicitly cites the text of the law and its 'insistence'.
tautology_reasoning: The rule 'only citizens can be adopted as citizens' is largely tautological.

verbatim: ['It is expedient, to, that you should uphold the law which permits us to adopt children and to dispose wisely of our property, reflecting that for men who are childless this law takes the place of children; for it is owing this law that both kinsmen and those who are not related take greater care of each other.']
reasoning: The speaker describes a general Greek legal norm (specifically applicable in Aegina and Siphnos) that allows men without biological children to adopt an heir to preserve their household and transfer their estate.
materiality_reasoning: Appointing heirs allows for the distribution of tangible estates and wealth.
generality_reasoning: Foundational societal axis involving inheritance and family preservation.
persistence_reasoning: The appointment of an heir has permanent effects on the estate's future.
immutability_reasoning: Childlessness is a biological fact or state at a given moment.
contemporarity_reasoning: Describes active laws regarding disposal of property.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The author encourages upholding an existing, functional law.
tautology_reasoning: Being childless does not naturally imply the legal right to adopt.

verbatim: ['Of this marriage were born Sopolis, Thrasylochus, and a daughter, who is my wife. These were the only legitimate children left by Thrasyllus and he made these his heirs when he died.']
reasoning: The text establishes that legitimate offspring (born of a recognized marriage) are the recognized legal heirs to their father's estate, contrasting them with children from informal unions.
materiality_reasoning: Inheriting property is a primary mode of material wealth transfer.
generality_reasoning: Inheritance is a foundational societal right.
persistence_reasoning: Inheritance is a lifelong, structural transfer of status and assets.
immutability_reasoning: Legitimacy is a birth-fixed status defined by lineage.
contemporarity_reasoning: Discusses contemporary family law and inheritance practices.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reported as an uncontested legal fact of the marriage.
tautology_reasoning: Legitimacy is often defined by the right to inherit; thus tautological.

verbatim: ['He became an itinerant soothsayer, lived in many cities, and was intimate with several women, some of whom had children whom he never even recognized as legitimate... When he had acquired a large fortune and yearned for his fatherland, he left this woman and the others as well.']
reasoning: The text indicates that children born out of wedlock (not recognized as legitimate) had no claim to the father's fortune or lineage, as Thrasyllus was able to abandon them and their mothers without legal obligation to include them in his estate.
materiality_reasoning: Exclusion from inheritance is a major material deprivation.
generality_reasoning: Inheritance exclusion is a foundational societal axis.
persistence_reasoning: Loss of inheritance rights is typically lifelong and structural.
immutability_reasoning: Illegitimacy is a birth-fixed status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to contemporary legal consequences of non-recognition.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Narrates the factual outcome of non-recognition.
tautology_reasoning: Illegitimacy is essentially defined by lack of inheritance/recognition rights.

resource: Exemption from the hazards of fighting
verbatim: ['Furthermore, the cultivation of practical wisdom may also reasonably be attributed to Busiris. For example, he saw to it that from the revenues of the sacrifices the priests should acquire affluence, but self-control through the purifications prescribed by the laws, and leisure by exemption from the hazards of fighting and from all work.']
reasoning: Isocrates describes the Egyptian caste system where priests are specifically exempted from military service and manual labor to focus on religious and intellectual pursuits.
materiality_reasoning: Exemption from military hazards and work affects bodily safety and effort.
generality_reasoning: Exemption from labor and war is a broad structural privilege.
persistence_reasoning: Priestly status and its exemptions are lifelong in this context.
immutability_reasoning: Hereditary priesthood is a structural birth-status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Describes Egyptian customs from a legendary/historical distance.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports laws as established facts of the Egyptian system.
tautology_reasoning: Priesthood is a role; military exemption is a specific legal corollary.

verbatim: ['For example, he saw to it that from the revenues of the sacrifices the priests should acquire affluence, but self-control through the purifications prescribed by the laws, and leisure by exemption from the hazards of fighting and from all work.']
reasoning: The text establishes a rule where the economic surplus from religious sacrifices is legally designated for the upkeep and wealth of the priestly class.
materiality_reasoning: Affluence and sacrifice revenues are tangible wealth.
generality_reasoning: Specific to the economic rights of a religious caste.
persistence_reasoning: Professional affluence is a lifelong structural condition.
immutability_reasoning: Egyptian priesthood is a fixed structural role.
contemporarity_reasoning: Discusses the quasi-mythical Busiris and his foundation of Egypt.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes the system's economic logic as an established fact.
tautology_reasoning: Being a priest does not inherently define one as 'affluent' by revenue.

verbatim: ['For instance, the provision that no citizen fit for military service could leave the country without official authorization, the meals taken in common, and the training of their bodies; furthermore, the fact that lacking none of the necessities of life, they do not neglect the edicts of the State, and that none engage in any other crafts, but that all devote themselves to arms and warfare, all these practices they have taken from Egypt']
reasoning: Isocrates references the well-known Spartan (Lacedaemonian) legal restriction (xenelasia/travel control) where soldiers could not travel abroad without state permission.
materiality_reasoning: Freedom of movement is a core material liberty.
generality_reasoning: Restriction on travel is a broad category of civil control.
persistence_reasoning: Restriction lasts while the citizen is of military age.
immutability_reasoning: Being 'fit for military service' is an age-based biological grouping.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to well-known Spartan practices of the author's own time.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Identifies a specific 'provision' as an established fact.
tautology_reasoning: Military fitness does not automatically define legal travel restrictions.

verbatim: ['but if we should wish to adopt the laws of the Egyptians which prescribe that some must work and that the rest must protect the property of the workers, we should all possess our own goods and pass our days in happiness.']
reasoning: The author describes a specific legal division of labor in Egypt where the military/protector class is legally bound to protect the property and earnings of the laboring class.
materiality_reasoning: Protection of physical goods and property is highly material.
generality_reasoning: Property protection is a broad foundational axis.
persistence_reasoning: Provides a life-long structural arrangement for economic safety.
immutability_reasoning: Work/laborer status is a durable occupation-based group.
contemporarity_reasoning: Describes idealised/historical Egyptian laws.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Isocrates proposes adopting these laws, implying a mix of fact and wish.
tautology_reasoning: Being a worker does not definitionally include being protected by soldiers.

verbatim: ['Including in all classes the right numbers for the best administration of the commonwealth, he gave orders that the same individuals should always engage in the same pursuits, because he knew that those who continually change their occupations never achieve proficiency in even a single one of their tasks']
reasoning: The text describes the Egyptian caste/guild system where individuals are legally mandated to remain in the same profession for life to ensure proficiency.
materiality_reasoning: The right (or lack thereof) to change jobs affects economic choices.
generality_reasoning: Focuses on the specific regulation of occupational mobility.
persistence_reasoning: The rule mandates that individuals stay in one job for life.
immutability_reasoning: Caste/guild membership in Egypt was a structural/hereditary status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Describes ancient Egyptian social structure.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports the 'orders' of Busiris as factual foundations of the state.
tautology_reasoning: Being a 'worker' does not inherently mean one is barred from switching.

verbatim: ['The older men Busiris appointed to have charge of the most important matters, but the younger he persuaded to forgo all pleasures and devote themselves to the study of the stars, to arithmetic, and to geometry']
reasoning: The rule establishes that political leadership and management of 'important matters' is reserved for individuals based on their age (seniority).
materiality_reasoning: Charge of important matters equals control over state resources.
generality_reasoning: Political power is a foundational societal axis.
persistence_reasoning: Lasts from the onset of 'old age' until death.
immutability_reasoning: Age is a biologically fixed, immutable category at any given time.
contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the legendary foundation of the Egyptian state.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Narrated as a specific appointment/law by the founder.
tautology_reasoning: Old age is not defined by the possession of political power.

verbatim: ['I have come before you to give my counsels on the war against the barbarians and on concord among ourselves. [...] so long as conditions go on as before, and what has been said about them is inadequate, is it not our duty to scan and study this question, the right decision of which will deliver us from our mutual warfare, our present confusion, and our greatest ills?']
reasoning: Isocrates is exercising the established right of Athenian citizens (isegoria) to address the public on matters of state policy, specifically regarding war and domestic peace.
materiality_reasoning: Addressing the assembly has direct effects on laws and state wealth.
generality_reasoning: Political speech (isegoria) is a foundational civic right.
persistence_reasoning: The right to address the assembly is a lifelong citizen privilege.
immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a hard-to-change legal birth-status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Isocrates is currently performing the action in the Athenian context.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Verbatim describes the speaker exercising an accepted duty/right.
tautology_reasoning: Civic belonging does not tautologically include the right to speak.

verbatim: ['it adapts itself both to those who lack means and to those who wish to enjoy the means which they possess, and that it fails to be of service neither to those who are prosperous nor to those who are unfortunate in their own cities; nay, both classes find with us what they desire, the former the most delightful pastimes, the latter the securest refuge.']
reasoning: The text describes the Athenian polity as providing services and material safety (refuge) for the poor and entertainment for the rich, reflecting the system of theorika and social support for citizens.
materiality_reasoning: Public subsidies (theorika/refuge) are tangible material resources.
generality_reasoning: Access to welfare/safety reflects a broad category of state service.
persistence_reasoning: Subsidies are ongoing but dependent on the city's prosperity.
immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a structural birth-status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the benefits of the current Athenian system.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports these services as a factual adaptation of the city to its people.
tautology_reasoning: Being a citizen does not define one as a recipient of subsidies.

verbatim: ['it is disgraceful for us, who in our private life think the barbarians are fit only to be used as household slaves, to permit by our public policy so many of our allies to be enslaved by them']
reasoning: The author contrasts the status of free allies with 'household slaves' (oiketai), implying that slaves are inherently subject to the mastery and physical coercion of others as a standard societal rule.
materiality_reasoning: Protection from corporal punishment/slavery is a bodily entitlement.
generality_reasoning: Freedom from physical coercion is a foundational societal right.
persistence_reasoning: Slave status is generally lifelong and structural.
immutability_reasoning: Slavery is a hard-to-change legal status (requires manumission).
contemporarity_reasoning: Discusses common contemporary Greek attitudes toward household slaves.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports the practice of enslaving barbarians as a matter of fact.
tautology_reasoning: Slavery is definitionally the lack of freedom and physical autonomy.

verbatim: ['considering it an outrage that the many should be subject to the few, [...] that, furthermore, in a fatherland which belongs to all in common some should hold the place of masters, others of aliens, and that men who are citizens by birth should be robbed by law of their share in the government.']
reasoning: Isocrates criticizes oligarchies for making some residents feel like 'aliens' (metoikoi) in their own land, which confirms the standard rule that actual aliens (Metics) are legally barred from participating in the government.
materiality_reasoning: Exclusion from public office deprives one of political agency.
generality_reasoning: Holding office is a foundational societal axis.
persistence_reasoning: Metic status (excluded from office) is usually lifelong in Athens.
immutability_reasoning: Metic status is a durable social identity, though changeable by move.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to the legal realities of political participation in current polities.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes the legal robbing of government shares as a known fact.
tautology_reasoning: Being an alien resident does not definitionally include a ban on office.

verbatim: ['at the celebration of the Mysteries, the Eumolpidae and the Kerykes, because of our hatred of the Persians, give solemn warning to the other barbarians also, even as to men guilty of murder, that they are for ever banned from the sacred rites.']
reasoning: The text explicitly describes a formal religious rule enforced by the priestly families (Eumolpidae/Kerykes) at the Eleusinian Mysteries banning barbarians from participation.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
verbatim: ['If sedition and strife and divisions have arisen on my account, I will depart, I will go away whithersoever you will, and I will obey the commands of the people; only let the flock of Christ have peace with the presbyters set over it.']
reasoning: The text describes an established hierarchical order where 'presbyters' are 'set over' the 'flock' (the community). Clement presents this as a normative rule that should not be disrupted by sedition.
materiality_reasoning: Political power over a community represents concrete social and institutional authority.
generality_reasoning: Authority over a 'flock' spans many aspects of communal life.
persistence_reasoning: Authority persists for the duration of the presbyter's appointment.
immutability_reasoning: Presbyter is a social identity acquired through appointment.
contemporarity_reasoning: Clement refers to the contemporary church structure.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Asserts the hierarchy (presbyters 'set over') as the normative social order.
tautology_reasoning: Authority is an attribute granted to the role, not the definition of the role itself.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
verbatim: ['We know that many among ourselves have given themselves to bondage that they might ransom others. Many have delivered themselves to slavery, and provided food for others with the price they received for themselves.']
reasoning: Clement cites contemporary practices within his own community where individuals relinquish their freedom (selling themselves into slavery) to acquire resources (food or ransom) for other members.
materiality_reasoning: Freedom from bondage is a foundational material resource regarding bodily autonomy.
generality_reasoning: Slavery vs. freedom is a major structural axis of ancient society.
persistence_reasoning: Selling oneself into slavery is a permanent, lifelong status change.
immutability_reasoning: Christians are a religious community; slavery/freedom is a legal birth or structural status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Clement explicitly mentions 'among ourselves' in the present.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports specific cases of voluntary enslavement as factual occurrences.
tautology_reasoning: Being a Christian does not imply being a slave or relinquishing freedom.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
verbatim: ['Give concord and peace to us and to all that dwell on the earth, as thou didst give to our fathers who called on thee in holiness with faith and truth, and grant that we may be obedient to thy almighty and glorious name, and to our rulers and governors upon the earth.']
reasoning: Clement explicitly anchors the Christian life in obedience to 'rulers and governors upon the earth,' establishing a rule of civil submission to external political authorities.
materiality_reasoning: Obedience to governors limits individual autonomy and social action.
generality_reasoning: Submission to political authority is a foundational societal structure.
persistence_reasoning: The duty of submission is a permanent feature of a subject's life.
immutability_reasoning: Christians were a distinct, stable social/religious identity in Rome.
contemporarity_reasoning: This is a prayer for contemporary political stability and obedience.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim is a prayer and exhortation, expressing an ideal of obedience.
tautology_reasoning: Being a Christian does not analytically define one as obedient to secular rulers.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
verbatim: ['You therefore, who laid the foundation of the sedition, submit to the presbyters, and receive the correction of repentance, bending the knees of your hearts. Learn to be submissive, putting aside the boastful and the haughty self-confidence of your tongue.']
reasoning: This refers to the specific contemporary conflict in Corinth. The rule identifies that those who challenge the established order (the seditious) must yield their autonomy and status to the presbyters.
materiality_reasoning: Submitting to correction affects one's legal/social standing in the church.
generality_reasoning: Scoped to the specific context of institutional discipline and sedition.
persistence_reasoning: Refers to a specific episode of trial and correction.
immutability_reasoning: The 'seditious' are defined by their episodic actions against the church.
contemporarity_reasoning: Directly addresses the current conflict in the Corinthian church.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Clement's command ('submit') is a normative demand in an argument.
tautology_reasoning: Being seditious does not inherently mean one must submit to presbyters.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
verbatim: ['Thou, Master, hast given the power of sovereignty to them through thy excellent and inexpressible might, that we may know the glory and honour given to them by thee, and be subject to them, in nothing resisting thy will.']
reasoning: Clement explicitly identifies political rulers as having received sovereignty from God, and establishes a rule of subjection for the governed, defining the ruler's access to power and the subject's duty of non-resistance.
materiality_reasoning: Sovereignty is a physical and legal power to control territory and people.
generality_reasoning: Political sovereignty is a foundational axis of society.
persistence_reasoning: Rulership is a structural, long-term identity (monarchy/governorship).
immutability_reasoning: Rulership is a social/political role defined by holding power.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to the actual Roman 'rulers and governors' of the author's day.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes the distribution of power as a divinely-ordained social fact.
tautology_reasoning: Defining rulers as those who exercise sovereignty is tautological.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
verbatim: ['For thou, heavenly Master, king of eternity, hast given to the sons of men glory and honour and power over the things which are on the earth; do thou, O Lord, direct their counsels according to that which is good and pleasing before thee.']
reasoning: The text describes a general rule governing the human species ('the sons of men'), granting them the resource of authority or 'power' over the material world.
materiality_reasoning: Power over earthly things implies control over tangible property and material resources.
generality_reasoning: The power is described as dominion over all things on the earth, a foundational axis of existence.
persistence_reasoning: The rule describes a structural, lifelong condition for the human species.
immutability_reasoning: The group 'sons of men' refers to the human species by birth/lineage.
contemporarity_reasoning: Clement describes the current divinely ordained order of the world in his time.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The claim is framed within a prayer, mixing theological assertion with a description of perceived reality.
tautology_reasoning: Being human does not inherently entail 'dominion' over the earth (it is a granted right).

perseus_title: The Epistle to the Philippians of Saint Polycarp
verbatim: ['next teach our wives to remain in the faith given to them, and in love and purity, tenderly loving their husbands in all truth, and loving all others equally in all chastity, and to educate their children in the fear of God.']
reasoning: Polycarp outlines specific behavioral and social restrictions for wives within the Christian community, specifically mandating 'chastity' in their dealings with others and 'purity' in relation to their husbands, establishing a normative rule of restricted social/sexual autonomy.
materiality_reasoning: Chastity and restricted association have direct social and physical consequences for bodily autonomy.
generality_reasoning: Faithfulness and restricted association cover a broad category of social and domestic life.
persistence_reasoning: Marital fidelity requirements last for the entire duration of a legal marriage.
immutability_reasoning: Gender is a birth-fixed biological/social category.
contemporarity_reasoning: Polycarp is prescribing contemporary moral and social conduct for his community.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim is explicitly hortatory ('teach our wives to...'), stating an ideal rather than a fact.
tautology_reasoning: Being a woman or wife does not analytically require the removal of free association.

perseus_title: The Epistle to the Philippians of Saint Polycarp
verbatim: ['Likewise also let the younger men be blameless in all things; caring above all for purity, and curbing themselves from all evil; for it is good to be cut off from the lust of the things in the world, because every lust warreth against the Spirit, and neither fornicators nor the effeminate nor sodomites shall inherit the Kingdom of God, nor they who do iniquitous things. Wherefore it is necessary to refrain from all these things, and to be subject to the presbyters and deacons as to God and Christ.']
reasoning: The text explicitly commands younger men to be 'subject' to the presbyters (elders) and deacons, establishing a hierarchical rule based on age and ecclesiastical office.
materiality_reasoning: Subjectivity to elders involves concrete subjection to the legal and disciplinary authority of another.
generality_reasoning: Subjection to officials covers a wide range of social, legal, and behavioral outcomes.
persistence_reasoning: This status is bounded to a life stage ('younger men') and ends with transition to elderhood.
immutability_reasoning: Age is a biologically fixed status at any given moment in time.
contemporarity_reasoning: Polycarp describes the contemporary hierarchical structure of the early Church.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The text is an exhortation ('let the younger men...') rather than an empirical observation.
tautology_reasoning: Youth does not inherently mean subjection to specific ecclesiastical offices like deacons.

perseus_title: The Epistle to the Philippians of Saint Polycarp
verbatim: ['And let the presbyters also be compassionate, merciful to all, bringing back those that have wandered, caring for all the weak, neglecting neither widow, nor orphan nor poor, but ever providing for that which is good before God and man, refraining from all wrath, respect of persons, unjust judgment, being far from all love of money, not quickly believing evil of any, not hasty in judgment, knowing that we all owe the debt of sin.']
reasoning: The author defines the rules of conduct for those holding the office of presbyter, specifically stripping them of the right to act with 'wrath' or 'unjust judgment' while mandating care for the vulnerable.
materiality_reasoning: The right to judge and manage communal resources (widows/orphans) has significant material outcomes.
generality_reasoning: Specifically tied to the institutional role and procedural conduct of a church official.
persistence_reasoning: Duties and restrictions persist for the duration of the long-term ecclesiastical office.
immutability_reasoning: Presbyter status is a social/religious identity acquired through selection and training.
contemporarity_reasoning: Polycarp outlines the roles and rules for current church leadership.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim is normative ('let the presbyters also be...'), describing how they should behave.
tautology_reasoning: Being a presbyter does not definitionally include being 'compassionate' or 'not hasty' in judgment.

perseus_title: The Epistle to the Philippians of Saint Polycarp
verbatim: ['Likewise must the deacons be blameless; before his righteousness, as the servants of God and Christ and not of man, not slanderers, not double-tongued, not lovers of money, temperate in all things, compassionate, careful, walking according to the truth of the Lord, who was the servant of all.']
reasoning: As a rule of the community, deacons are explicitly barred from being 'lovers of money' and must prioritize communal service over personal material interests.
materiality_reasoning: The restriction on 'lovers of money' directly impacts the deacon's financial and material accumulation.
generality_reasoning: Narrowly scoped to the financial and ethical conduct of a specific church office.
persistence_reasoning: Restrictions hold for the duration of the deacon's term/career in office.
immutability_reasoning: Deacon status is a social identity acquired through appointment.
contemporarity_reasoning: This reflects the contemporary organizational rules of Polycarp's community.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The text is an ethical command ('must the deacons be...') rather than a factual census.
tautology_reasoning: A deacon is an officer; 'not wanting money' is a restriction on that role, not its definition.

perseus_title: The Epistle to the Philippians of Saint Polycarp
resource: Right to be part of the community
verbatim: ['For eyeryone who does not confess that Jesus Christ lias come in the flesh is an anti-Christ; and whosoever does not confess the testimony of the Cross is of the devil: and whosoever perverts the oracles of the Lord for his own lusts, and says that there is neither resurrection nor judgment,—this man is the first-born of Satan.']
reasoning: The text sets a boundary based on religious confession; those who deviate from the core dogma are labeled as 'anti-Christ' or 'of the devil', which in the context of this letter serves as a rule for their social and ritual exclusion.
materiality_reasoning: Exclusion from the community (excommunication) results in loss of social support and legal standing.
generality_reasoning: Communal inclusion/exclusion represents a foundational societal axis for the early Christians.
persistence_reasoning: Exclusion persists as long as the theological deviation (belief) remains unrepented.
immutability_reasoning: Heretic status is defined by adherence to a specific belief, which is durable but changeable.
contemporarity_reasoning: Polycarp is identifying current individuals and groups within his own society for exclusion.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The author identifies these people as 'first-born of Satan,' a factual label for social exclusion in this group.
tautology_reasoning: Holding specific dogmatic views is not definitionally the same as being 'socially excluded'.
